coredata.dc:title: Lithium-ion battery impedance as a battery temperature indicator

coredata.dc:description: In this study, we propose and evaluate the performance of an impedance-based model for battery temperature estimation. We used four different datasets including Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) experiments measured at different conditions, including variations in technology, temperatures, State-of-Charge (SoC) and State-of-Health (SoH). One of the datasets collects new EIS data coming from new experimental tests. By means of a correlation analysis, we select the reactance of the impedance in the frequency range from 100 to 1000Hz as the most suitable temperature indicator. Then, we evaluate the temperature estimation performance of some linear and exponential models with a cross-validation approach, using the reactance measured at a single frequency point considering also the effects of SoC and SoH. A further contribution of this manuscript is the presentation of a dataset including EIS experiments under multiple temperature and SoC conditions, that was acquired in our laboratories and published online for the scientific community. In this paper, we provide an accurate description of the tests performed.

head.ce:title: Lithium-ion battery impedance as a battery temperature indicator

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we propose and evaluate the performance of an impedance-based model for battery temperature estimation. We used four different datasets including Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) experiments measured at different conditions, including variations in technology, temperatures, State-of-Charge (SoC) and State-of-Health (SoH). One of the datasets collects new EIS data coming from new experimental tests. By means of a correlation analysis, we select the reactance of the impedance in the frequency range from 100 to 1000Hz as the most suitable temperature indicator. Then, we evaluate the temperature estimation performance of some linear and exponential models with a cross-validation approach, using the reactance measured at a single frequency point considering also the effects of SoC and SoH. A further contribution of this manuscript is the presentation of a dataset including EIS experiments under multiple temperature and SoC conditions, that was acquired in our laboratories and published online for the scientific community. In this paper, we provide an accurate description of the tests performed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data used in the paper are available online and referenced in the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are used in a wide range of applications, from consumer electronics to grid-connected battery energy storage systems. For instance, by the end of 2022, the global amount of installed capacity of new energy storage reached 45.7GW, from which 94.4% corresponded to Li-ion batteries [1,2]. Often in energy storage systems, high numbers of Li-ion cells are placed in small closed spaces. Such conditions represent a real safety hazard in case of thermal runaway.

Li-ion batteries require a battery management system (BMS) to ensure the battery works in its safe operating area. Every battery operating parameter must be kept between specific safety bounds by the BMS, and battery temperature is one of the most critical. On the one hand, working with low temperatures can lead to undesired reactions, such as loss of active material, lithium plating, and dendrite formation with the potential creation of internal short circuits [3]. On the other hand, operating at high temperatures may cause capacity degradation, solid electrolyte interface decomposition, and separator melting that eventually may trigger internal exothermic reactions with catastrophic effects, such as thermal runaways [4,5].

Accurate cell-level temperature modelling and tracking may help in an effective management of battery cells, by reducing the ageing effects, and by prolonging the battery life [6]. Implementing sensors and BMS that can detect early signs of failure, such as unusual temperature spikes or voltage anomalies, is a key approach. A high cell temperature could be an indicator of overvoltage, overcurrent, and/or short circuit conditions [7]. Advanced monitoring and control systems can initiate preventive measures, such as disconnecting affected cells or modules to prevent the escalation of the situation. With suitable detection schemes, it is possible to act early and prevent cell-level thermal runaways, or at least activate safety systems limiting the spread of runaways to neighbour cells [2].

The temperature in a battery pack is usually monitored using surface temperature sensors that should be optimally distributed throughout the pack [8]. Two major factors limit the monitoring of temperature through surface sensors: first, at the battery pack level, one sensor per cell is costly, due to the increased number of analog inputs and sensors in a real BMS [9]; second, under typical cell operating conditions, the surface and internal temperatures may differ considerably. The internal temperature cannot be measured directly with a sensor, calling for the estimation from surface measurements with sophisticated thermal and/or electrochemical models [10], or alternatively, with machine learning models [11].

Rosewater et al. suggest that, for prompt and effective fault detection, some safety-critical measurements should be provided, and their accuracy and acquisition delay should meet specific requirements. In particular, a temperature measurement accuracy within 2°C is required, and for safety-critical automation, the time between measurement and system controller actuation should not exceed 10s [12]. In this context, a temperature estimation based on an electrochemical impedance measurement seems a suitable option that meets such requirements.

Recent literature proposes the estimation of battery temperature from impedance measurements at specific frequencies, as an alternative addressing the limitations of approaches using surface temperature sensors. Du et al. discussed an online sensorless temperature measurement method for Li-ion batteries using look-up tables containing impedance and temperature pairs [13]. Beelen et al. compared the accuracy of several impedance-based temperature estimation approaches, and proposed a method based on a measurement of the impedance at 50Hz as the one with the highest estimation accuracy [14]. Both Beelen et al. and Du et al. used as inputs for their models the real and imaginary parts of the impedance. Considering that the impedance real part is highly sensitive to ageing, it cannot be considered as a reliable temperature indicator if such dependency is not compensated. Despite this, such a dependence was not taken into account. Furthermore, accurate measurements of the real part of the impedance may be challenging, as its value, on the order of mΩ, is often on the same order of magnitude as the battery system cables and connections. Unstable connections during battery operation may introduce parasitic resistances that affect the measurement of the real part of the battery impedance.

Haussmann et al. used the battery impedance amplitude at 500Hz for onboard temperature estimation, and they showed that the influence of the State-of-Charge (SoC) on the impedance was negligible at that frequency [15]. The impedance amplitude varies with State-of-Health (SoH) and parasitic ohmic impedances of connections, making necessary the periodical calibration of models based on the amplitude. Srinivasan et al. showed that it is possible to predict and prevent thermal runaway by monitoring the changes in the phase of the battery impedance [16]. The model they developed was very helpful for runaway detection, but every cell required its own calibration curve. Bhoir et al. demonstrated that the phase of the impedance at specific frequencies is linearly dependent on the battery temperature, but such range is also dependent on SoH [17]. Zhu et al. used both phase and magnitude of the impedance for temperature estimation, but the phase acquisition requires a phase sensitive detector that may not be easy to integrate onboard a BMS [18]. Hussein et al. implemented a sensorless measurement technique of internal battery temperature based on impedance phase with a high level of accuracy, but their approach relies on identifying the frequency at which the imaginary part of the impedance is zero, which is often over 1 kHz. Measuring the phase at such frequency range requires sampling frequencies beyond the capabilities of commercial BMS [19]. McCarthy et al. developed a model for temperature estimation based on a single-point measurement of the imaginary part at 200Hz [20] during battery discharge. Their model is composed of linear models operating in two reduced temperature intervals: one from 10 to 35°C, and the other from 35 to 55°C. Furthermore, every cell required a specific calibration and set of parameters, limiting the applicability of the model.

In their work, Ouyang et al. found that the imaginary part of the impedance at 263.03 and 524.81Hz is insensitive to SoC and SoH variations, and therefore it looked suitable for temperature detection [21]. Despite this, their model was trained on only 4 NMC cells having the same rated parameters, and, as it is the case in most of the existing literature, they analysed reduced uniform sets of battery cells. This aspect limits the applicability of the results because the model specifically fitted the battery under test, which may deeply differ from other cells.

In this work, we aim to address such limitations by proposing and validating a novel data-driven approach for temperature estimation based on a wide set of impedance measurements from various types of battery cells. The idea is to propose a battery temperature model that requires low computational performance, and could easily be integrated onboard a battery monitor or BMS for the internal temperature estimation of every cell in large battery packs, without adding one temperature sensor per cell. We use four databases for model training, including Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) data at various temperatures: three are taken from the literature, and the fourth was specifically acquired in one of our laboratories for this work within a collaboration between the University of Salerno and Kiel University. Data are made publicly available as supplementary material to this paper.

The datasets contain data corresponding to cells with different technologies and capacities, tested under multiple temperatures, SoC and SoH conditions. Despite such diversity, this work exploits the common properties among cells, leading to the identification of a global thermal model. Low-temperature data reach -10°C, which is the most difficult temperature range to model, because the impedance is very sensitive to temperature, often requiring non-linear models. Furthermore, we included data considering the effect of a charge and discharge current during the impedance measurement, by considering EIS experiments acquired under such conditions during the identification of the best features for temperature estimation and the evaluation of the models. To the best of the author’s knowledge, no existing literature considers the latter effects, making them important novelties of this paper.

In order to develop the data-driven thermal models, some impedance-based temperature indicators are investigated to find the ones with low sensitivity to SoC, SoH, and physical parameter changes among different cells. A correlation analysis of the battery impedance spectrum with temperature shows which indicator performs best. This is another of the key contributions of the paper.

The paper is organised as follows. In Section 2 we present the UNISA-CAU dataset 1
1 UNISA stands for “Università degli Studi di Salerno” (University of Salerno), CAU stands for “Christian-Albrecht Universität zu Kiel” (Kiel University). ; in Section 3 we describe the other datasets that are merged with the UNISA-CAU dataset and are also included in our analysis; then, in Section 4 we discuss the selection of the best feature for temperature estimation and the frequency range of interest based on a correlation analysis between the impedance and temperature data; in Section 5, we propose and evaluate the performance of several models for temperature estimation; in Section 6 we discuss the performance of the selected temperature models; in Section 7 we describe the main effect of changes in the battery states on the impedance, and we present some perspectives; and finally in Section 8 we draw some conclusions.
In this section, we describe the UNISA-CAU dataset and the experiment performed for data acquisition.

Using the facilities of the Laboratory for Reliable Battery-based Energy Conversion, 2
2 BAEW – Labor für zuverlässige batteriegestützte Energiewandlung Link: https://www.pe.tf.uni-kiel.de/en/laboratory/battery-laboratory. Kiel University, we tested four Li-ion cylindrical Nickel Oxide (NO)-based battery cells, manufactured by Panasonic, model NCR18650B, with a rated capacity of
C = 3300
m Ah
. The batteries were subjected to reference charge–discharge and EIS tests under multiple temperature and SoC conditions.

Fig. 1(a) shows the setup used for the experiments. The setup includes: a climate chamber Espec ARL-1100 for ambient temperature control; a battery tester by Arbin Instruments, model MYx Sys, for battery characterisation and spectroscopy stimulation; an Interface 5000E potentiostat/galvanostat/ZRA by Gamry Instruments, for EIS measurements; an Arbin AUX-PT100 multichannel temperature data acquisition system for ambient and battery surface temperature measurement; and a personal computer to control the setup. Fig. 1(b) shows the cells under test. The four cells were mounted on the test bed with universal 4-wire cylindrical battery holders, and a temperature sensor was placed in the middle of the side surface of each cell with thermal paste. This configuration enables simultaneous testing of all four cells.

Two types of tests were performed simultaneously on all cells at various ambient temperatures
T
amb
, from -10°C to 40°C, with steps of 5°C. The first test aimed to evaluate the cell average capacity as a function of temperature. This was useful for the evaluation of SoC while performing spectroscopy, as it enabled the computing of SoC with reference to an actual capacity rather than to the nominal one. The second test is an EIS at various temperatures and SoC levels.

For each
T
amb
, to extract the reference capacity of each cell, we performed three complete Constant Current (CC)-Constant Voltage (CV) charge/discharge cycles at C/2, with rest time
t
rest
= 30
min
between charge and discharge. The following parameters were used for the tests: an upper cut-off voltage of
V
up
= 4.2
V
(from the cell’s datasheet), a lower cut-off voltage of
V
lw
= 2.8
V
, and a cut-off current of C/10 for the CV steps. In the battery’s datasheet the lower cut-off voltage
V
lw
is reported as 2.5V, but we used
V
lw
= 2.8
V
to avoid deep discharge at C/2 at very low temperatures, which may be outside the safe operating area declared by the manufacturer. This decision slightly impacts the real capacity of the battery, reducing it by a few percentage points, but it significantly benefits the battery SoH, by reducing stress and early ageing.

The reference discharge capacity of the cells
Q
ref
at each
T
amb
was extracted by computing for each cell the average between the three discharge capacity values achieved in each discharge, and then averaging again the resulting four values. The average capacity
Q
ref
varies from 3.319Ah at 40°C to 2.639Ah at -10°C, a reduction that corresponds to 20% of the nominal capacity. The values of
Q
ref
are used for SoC evaluation. Appendix A reports the results in detail.

The second set of experiments consists of a sequence of 3 EIS tests for each cell (12 spectra per SoC per temperature), in a frequency range from 10kHz down to 0.1Hz, at SoC values from 90% down to 10%, with steps of 10%. These experiments are repeated for all temperatures
T
amb
= 40
°C
,
35
°C
, … ,
-10
°C
.

For each temperature, the experiment is the following. First, the experiment is initialised by setting the temperature of the climate chamber at
T
amb
, leaving the cell, charged at
V
0
= 3.7
V
, at
T
amb
for at least
t
rest
= 1
h
; then, the cell is fully charged in CC-CV mode at C/2, leaving it at rest for
t
rest
= 60
min
. After the initialisation, we repeat the following two steps:

ce:section.ce:para:
These two steps are repeated, testing the cell at SoC values from 90% down to 10%. Finally, the cell is completely discharged in CC-CV mode, and then charged again in CC mode at C/2 until reaching
V
0
= 3.7
V
. After that, we change
T
amb
.

The maximum cumulative error on SoC at the last discharge step is always lower than 2%, for all
T
amb
. Selected data are shown in detail in Appendix A.
We collected all available data in a database, to which we will refer to in the following as the UNISA-CAU database. The database is available online [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Online datasets description and data merge
ce:section.ce:para: For the analysis presented hereinafter, we use impedance data extracted from three different datasets available online in addition to the UNISA-CAU dataset. The three public datasets provide EIS measurements performed at different SoC values and temperatures, with zero offset current, as is the case for the UNISA-CAU data. Typically, each EIS experiment was performed after a rest time of at least 30min.

The first dataset was recorded at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, USA, and corresponds to a set of characterisation experiments for a 2.9Ah Nickel-Cobalt-Aluminium (NCA) Panasonic 18650PF cylindrical cell, tested at the beginning of its life. The dataset includes EIS tests performed at different SoC and ambient temperature. The temperature is controlled by a thermal chamber, and the tests are performed with a 25A, 18V Digatron Firing Circuits Universal Battery Tester [23].

The second dataset, from the University of Loughborough, UK, includes EIS data from experiments on a single 3Ah NCA 18650 cylindrical cell at different SoC, SoH, and temperatures. The cell was connected to a Gamry 5000E potentiostat in a four-wire setup (Kelvin connection) for cycling and EIS tests. The cell and holder were placed in a polypropylene cylinder which was then filled with dielectric oil (Castrol E2259I/033 A) until the entire cell was submerged. The cylinder was then placed in an oil bath where the oil temperature was regulated using a Julabo CORIO CD immersion circulator [24].

The third dataset was collected by the Laboratory LICIT-ECO7, Gustave-Eiffel University, France. It contains EIS spectra measured on three 2.6Ah Samsung ICR18650-26J cells, which were tested in a thermal chamber with a Biologic potentiostat. For each cell, EIS measurements have been performed at ambient temperatures from 5 to 40°C. In addition, the experiments are repeated with an offset current of 0.65A and -0.65A, that is C/4, used to continuously change the SoC. In this case, the EIS spectra are taken with no rest time. EIS experiments were performed at variable SoC and temperature [25].

Table 1 summarises the main information on each dataset. All batteries are 18650 cylindrical cells having similar capacity. There are some differences in the cell chemistries, but all of them are made of nickel, cobalt and/or aluminium oxides.

Fig. 2 shows some impedance spectra taken from all the four datasets. Each plot shows spectra taken at 50% SoC at various temperatures; each plot includes curves from the same cell tested at fresh state. The EIS data are characterised by a depressed semi-arc in the middle-frequency range, as expected for Li-ion cells. All plots show that, when the temperature increases, the width and height of the semi-arc reduce. Moreover, for three out of four sets, the plots shift towards the left. Often, these two trends indicate a decrease of the charge transfer and ohmic resistances, and an increase of the double-layer capacitance [26]. The data from the Gustave-Eiffel dataset do not exhibit the typical shift to the left in the plots. As shown in Fig. 2, the EIS appears randomly distributed along the real-axis. Several reasons could cause this unexpected behaviour: for instance, an instability of cable connections, or an early ageing phenomenon related to a test campaign using an unsorted vector of temperatures. Despite this anomalous behaviour, linear Kramers-Kroning tests applied to the whole Gustave-Eiffel data yielded fitting residuals below 1% without any apparent bias, for both real and imaginary parts of the impedance. According to the literature, this results suggest the validity of the data [27]. Additionally, despite the anomalous offset on the real part, the reactance’s dependence with temperature is as expected, as shown in Fig. 3. Therefore, we consider the Gustave-Eiffel dataset valid for further analyses.

We merge data from all four datasets to build a single EIS dataset for the model training. We take the data corresponding to temperatures between -10°C and 40°C, SoC values between 100% and 0%, and SoH values between 100% and 91%.

Considering that the EIS tests are typically performed on the cell after rests of at least 30min, we assume that the temperature is uniform throughout the cell, neglecting the differences between the surface and internal temperatures. Under this assumption, in the following sections, we tackle the problem of using EIS data for temperature estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation analysis between impedance features and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Using the whole EIS spectrum for temperature estimation could be quite ineffective and time-consuming, because several minutes or tens of minutes could be necessary to acquire a single spectrum, and, obviously, such an approach is unpractical for real-time applications [28].

To estimate the temperature from impedance data, we propose to look for a single frequency point well correlated to temperature, and with low sensitivity to SoC and SoH, allowing a fast data acquisition and temperature estimation. Therefore, we analyse four features extracted from the impedance Z : the impedance amplitude
| Z |
; the phase
φ = arg
{ Z }
, which is the delay of the current with respect to the voltage; the real part
R = ℜ
{ Z }
, which is the resistance; and the imaginary part
X = ℑ
{ Z }
, called reactance.

We use the Pearson correlation coefficient,
r
ab
, to identify the frequency range in which the battery impedance exhibits the strongest correlation with temperature. Such a coefficient quantifies the correlation between the two vectors a and b of N elements, and it is defined as:

r

, the higher the linear dependency between the variables.

We compute the Pearson coefficient between each of the aforementioned impedance features and temperature. The variations of SoC and SoH among the impedance data introduce noise to the temperature-impedance correlation, with a major severity in specific frequency ranges. For example, Fig. 3 shows for the four datasets all the plots for the reactance as a function of frequency. For each frequency, the Pearson correlation coefficient r between the reactance and the temperature is computed and then plotted on Fig. 3 with a dashed line. The correlation is high (
| r |
> 0 . 95
) for frequencies over some hundreds of Hertz, and reaches its peak between 500 and 1000Hz. It is worth noting that the frequency range with the largest
| r |
does not match the range presenting the largest variation of the imaginary part with temperature, with the later occurring at the lowest frequencies.

We performed the same analysis on all available data, computing the Pearson correlation coefficient with temperature for all impedance features to compare their effectiveness as temperature indicators. In this way, we obtain four curves for each feature, one per dataset. To merge the results, we interpolate the four curves on a fine frequency grid, and, for each feature, compute the minimum value of
| r |
among the four. The results are shown in compact form in Fig. 4. The phase and the reactance reach the highest values of
| r |
, well over 0.95, around 977Hz and 613Hz, respectively.

In the frequency range 100Hz–1kHz, the Pearson coefficient index is over 0.93, and we consider it promising to further analyse the link between the impedance features and the temperature. Frequencies beyond 1kHz would require high measurement performances, not suitable for low-cost measurement systems such as those found in a commercial BMS. While, for frequencies lower than 100Hz, the impact of SoH and SoC on impedance is too high. Therefore, we prefer not to extend the range further.
At fixed temperatures, the reactance X is characterised by a lower dispersion due to SoC and SoH variations than the phase φ , making it more promising for reliable temperature estimation.

Additionally, a subset of the data from the Gustave-Eiffel dataset allows us to analyse the effect of offset currents (both in charge or discharge at C/4) superimposed on the EIS stimulus, at least as a preliminary study. Fig. 5 shows the reactance X at various temperatures, using a gradient colourmap. For each temperature, we plot data from both tests: with offset (dashed and dash-dotted lines) and without offset (solid line). The effect of the offset is small at middle frequencies (from 100 to1000Hz): the difference in X is less than 0.5%. Conversely, at low and high frequencies, the impact of the offset is higher, overtaking 15%. Therefore, operating in the middle frequency range, the effect of the offset current (0.5%) is negligible with respect to the effect of temperature, which causes a 10% variation for a 1°C temperature change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature model based on reactance features
ce:section.ce:para: We use a cross-validation approach to assess the performance of some linear and non-linear models for the estimation of the temperature, named T , in the range from -10 to 40°C. The estimation is based on the imaginary part X of the impedance Z , which is the reactance. First, we evaluate the quality of the estimation without information on SoC and SoH, and then we check how it can be improved by including SoC or SoH.

Obviously, the model (12) (or, alternatively, (9)) is the most general one, and (10)–(11) are particular cases of (12), with some coefficients set to zero. However, we decided to treat them separately, because such simpler models do not require the computation of additional variables like SoC and SoH, with lower computational burden. Such characteristic is particularly useful in applications where the models are implemented onboard in a BMS.
We considered the six models (7)–(12) at the 8 selected frequencies presented in the set (5), for a total of 48 models.

It is worth to remark that the phase φ of the impedance was also considered for temperature estimation, but the temperature estimation based on the reactance showed better estimation performance in all tests. Therefore, in the following, we only report the results of the estimation based on the reactance.

A total of 2064 spectra are available for temperatures in the range of interest among the datasets, characterised by specific values of T , SoH, and SoC. For each spectrum, eight
x
f
values are extracted. So, for each frequency value, 2064 data points linking
x
f
to T , SoH, and SoC are available, and are randomly separated into training and test sets, according to the typical ratio 70%:30%.

We use the training set to perform cross-validation with a 10-fold approach for each model at a specific frequency value, with the aim of selecting the best performing models. Cross-validation is performed by splitting the training test into ten subsets: nine of them are used for the training, and the tenth one for the evaluation of both the mean squared error
MSE
k
, and the adjusted determination coefficient
R
adj , k

. The procedure is repeated ten times using a different subset for validation every time [29]. After cross-validation, we compute the average performance indicators of each model in the training set, extracting the root MSE (RMSE) as

. The cross-validation results are summarised in Table 2 for the five models with the lowest RMSE. For such models, in terms of

Models using the exponential relationship with
x

at middle frequency outperform all the others in terms of RMSE and

is the most promising one. The results also show that the information from SoC and SoH improves the fitting of the training set, reducing the effects linked to the spread of data at the same temperature for various SoC and SoH values.

According to the cross-validation analysis, the best-performing model based on a single impedance feature is exp(x) at 335Hz (position 5), while the best ones including information on SoC and SoH are the exp(x)+lin(SoC) and the exp(x)+lin(SoC+SoH) at 335Hz (position 1 and 2). We use those three models for the prediction of temperature by training them on the full training set, and validating them and assessing their estimation performance on data from the test set. It is worth noting that the test set has never been used in cross-validation.

The temperature estimation is performed using a model fitting in Matlab with the function Fit non-linear regression model ‘fitnlm’. The obtained results are summarised in Table 3 for the selected models. Here, we report on all the estimated parameters with p -values under 0.01, that is, they are significant to the 99% significance level. We also report the standard error of the parameters that represents the average amount in which the estimated parameters differ from their expected value. For most of the parameters in the models reported in Table 3, the ratio between the standard error and the parameter estimate is less than 0.1, which highlights the high robustness of each model. The performance indicators of the fit, RMSE and
R
adj
2
, are also summarised in Table 3. Fig. 7 shows all prediction results, linking the temperatures predicted through the impedance feature to the actual temperature for the three selected models.

The model ‘exp(x)’ at 335Hz has an rms error of 2.18°C. This result suggests that a single fast impedance measurement is enough to estimate the battery temperature within 2.18°C rms. The results are improved by adding a linear correction associated with SoC, achieving an rms error of 1.91°C. Such information, reduces the effect of the spread of predictions around the actual temperature, as we show in Fig. 7. The further addition of the correction associated with the SoH leads to an additional improvement, reducing the rms error to 1.86°C. Despite the interesting results, that would require a SoH estimator, rarely available on BMS, while the information on SoC is typically available.

The beneficial effect of the SoC contribution is particularly evident at high temperatures: the spread of the green circles is lower than that of the blue crosses, obtained using only
x
f
. The model ‘exp(x)+lin(SoC+SoH)’ has the best performance, with the lowest rms error and a determination coefficient approaching 1, meaning that the model can explain most of the variation in the temperature.

Fig. 8 shows the distribution of the residuals for the three selected models. The distribution suggests that the model errors are unbiased (demonstrated by the symmetry of the graph), and normally distributed.

The results obtained in Section 6 deserve a further discussion. In this section, we discuss the electrochemical principles that influence the results of the correlation analysis, the impact of battery state on the impedance, the model sensitivity with temperature, and, finally, we present some perspectives.

In large battery storage systems, thermal models should require low computational performance to estimate the temperature of thousands of cells. For this reason, a compromise should be found between model accuracy and complexity. Our models are suitable for cells with various properties and in various battery states. They require few parameters and very simple mathematical operations. Furthermore, some commercial BMS are already suitable for accurate impedance measurements, as reported in [28].

On the contrary, physics-based models, like electrothermal coupled models based on pseudo two-dimensional (P2D) models, can lead to more accurate results. They can also provide the three-dimensional temperature distribution throughout the battery, as in the case of [30], but they are more complex, requiring high computational resources for real-time computations, such as those ones of a personal computer [31], and their full parametrisation is often only possible if detailed fabrication information is available or by tear-down test [32]. Furthermore, physics-based modelling requires a deep understanding of battery electrochemistry, which is not always possessed by non-expert workers in commercial or industrial applications. In this context, impedance-based modelling is more suitable, as it enables optimal and safe battery management without requiring specialised expertise. Hence, although the impedance estimation brings the computational burden of the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), impedance-based models represent a valid alternative to physics-based models in view of their potential for integration onboard the next generation of BMS, despite the lower accuracy.

The flow chart in Fig. 9 represents the procedure for the on-board implementation of the model. Excluding the preliminary model training and the measurement of the reference impedance values that should be performed offline, the temperature measurement consists of three main steps: first, apply a sinusoidal current stimulation at
f = 335
Hz
; second, compute the FFT of the battery voltage (V) and current (I) signals, and derive the normalised reactance as:
(13)
x
f
=
1
x
0
ℑ
fft
( V )
fft
( I )
;
third, calculate the temperature using (10), (11), or (12), depending on what information is available. This three-step procedure is easy to integrate into existing BMS, and the time required for a single acquisition and estimation is low. For instance, a commercial battery monitor like L9963E from STMicroelectronics, controlled by a Pynq Z2 microcontroller, takes less than 5s for high-accuracy repeated measurement, as shown in [28].

Different features could be selected in impedance-based temperature estimation. Section 4 has proved that the phase and reactance have similar correlation with temperature, and some research articles used the impedance phase as the main feature for temperature estimation. In order to show that the selection of the type of feature has little impact on the on-board computational burden, we compare our approach with the one proposed by Bhoir et al. in [17], which is very similar to the one in Fig. 9. After a preliminary determination of the best frequency range, based on the analysis of the distribution of relaxation time and the model fitting, the temperature estimation consists of measuring the impedance phase at a single frequency point and using it as an input for their model. The time required by the temperature estimation is similar to that one of our approach, because the same steps are required. The main difference lies in the linearity of Bhoir’s model against the exponential model we adopt, and its impact on the computational time on modern microcontrollers is low. Nevertheless, the impedance phase onboard measurement is much more a challenging task than computing the reactance. Indeed, the phase could be measured in a direct way by integrating phase-sensitive detectors in the BMS [18], or computed from the FFT. In the first case, the measurement is very fast and does not require post-processing, but it may be an expensive solution, especially in large battery packs with several modules; in the second case, the impedance phase computation has lower accuracy than the reactance, because the signal-to-noise ratio of the phase is typically lower than the one of the reactance [28].

Our model is an example of how the changes of the EIS can be used as an indicator of the battery state. In particular, our model uses only the reactance measured at 335Hz. We performed a further analysis on the Loughborough dataset to understand the main electrochemical contribution to the measured impedance. Such analysis is done by fitting the parameters of an equivalent circuit model representing the impedance:

Ω
is the resistor for the ohmic resistances; third and fourth terms are the two Zarc elements for the charge-transfer and SEI contributions; and the fifth term is a Warburg element for the low frequency diffusions and migration effects. The results of parameter fitting at various SoH, SoC, and temperature levels on the Loughborough dataset are available in the supplementary material. For this qualitative analysis, we used only the Loughborough dataset because it is the only one accounting for significant SoH variations.

Fig. 10 shows that the characteristic frequency of the charge transfer contribution to the reactance of the impedance is around 20Hz, while the characteristic frequency of the SEI contribution is around 300Hz. The frequency used in the model is very close to the characteristic frequency of the SEI effect. For this reason, the impedance changes in our model are mainly correlated with the electric properties of the SEI layer, and partially correlated with charge transfer resistance and double-layer capacitance.

The main effect of temperature is the ion mobility increase and the electrolyte viscosity decrease with temperature. It involves an exponential increase in the charge transfer resistance that follows the Arrhenius equation [34,35]. Furthermore, the resistivity of the battery materials and, in particular, the resistivity of the SEI layer, is proportional to the temperature, which involves an impedance amplitude decrease as temperature increases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of SoC and SoH on impedance
ce:section.ce:para: Although the temperature is the main factor that influences the reactance change in the middle-high frequency range, SoC and SoH also partially affect it. The impact of SoC on battery impedance is mainly on the ohmic and charge transfer resistance. They have a bounce-back effect: the charge transfer semicircle decreases as the SoC increases; it reaches a minimum around 50–60 % SoC, and then it starts to increase again as SoC increases to 100%. So the resistances are high for both low and high SoC values, and this behaviour is caused by the reduced mobility of ions. Equivalent circuit elements plots in the supplementary materials are fully compatible with this behaviour.

The impedance also increases with SoH. The main reason is the degradation of the electroactive materials, the loss of lithium inventory, and the growth of the SEI layer on the anode, whose main effect is the appearance of a second depressed semicircle in the spectra [36,37]. Capacity is irreversibly lost due to the cyclable lithium trapped within the SEI. In addition, the SEI layer is less permeable to lithium ions than the electrolyte, therefore, the electrolyte remains blocked in pores, resulting in a consumption of the electrolyte solvent. These effects increase the overall impedance of cells, including both the charge transfer and the SEI resistances [38].

SoC and SoH mainly affect the charge transfer resistance and the double layer capacitance of the battery, involving valuable impedance changes in the corresponding Zarc element of the impedance. Such an element corresponds to the mid-low frequency range, and, for this reason, the correlation analysis with temperature provided higher correlation for higher frequencies. In our model, we also took into account SoC and SoH with a linear dependence. In this way, we mitigate SoC and SoH effects in the mid-high frequency range and improve the model accuracy, but the overall effect of SoH on the model performance is partially hidden, because only the Loughborough dataset contains SoH dependence. Therefore, the effect of SoH deserves further consideration.

To evaluate the overall SoH effect over the models ‘exp(x)+lin (SoC+SoH)’, we computed the RMSE for every SoH value of the Loughborough dataset. The resulting RMSE ranges from 3.15°C to 2.33°C, reducing the model accuracy of about 0.8°C, without compromising the usefulness of the model for battery temperature monitoring and thermal runaway detection.

In conclusion, changing the SoH from 1 to 0.9, the RMSE increase is about 0.8°C. Therefore, the linear model dependence on SoH is able to describe the ageing effect on SEI, which affects in a limited way the model accuracy.

It is worth noting that, at the frequency of 335Hz, the average sensitivity of the reactance to a reference variation of 1°C is 0.15mΩ. Such a sensitivity complies with the accuracy of some commercial BMS [28]. The peak of the correlation analysis is around 613Hz, and does not depend on the model used. Conversely, the frequency with the lowest RMSE for the estimated temperature is located around 335Hz, and this result is strongly dependent on which model is used. Therefore, such two numbers can differ from one another for the effects of SoC and SoH on the former, as discussed in the previous subsection.

Validating this model on large-capacity cells would represent a significant improvement, expanding its applicability to next-generation batteries designed for high-power applications, such as photovoltaic storage and automotive systems. These cells range in capacity from 40–50Ah for pouch and prismatic formats up to 200Ah for blade cells.

In order to perform a preliminary test of the performance of our models on high-capacity cells, we incorporate an additional dataset provided by Denso Corporation [39]. This dataset includes the results of 100 EIS tests on 50Ah prismatic NMC cells taken at 50% SoC, various SoH levels, and four temperatures ranging from -10°C to 25°C. This dataset differs significantly from the previous ones, especially in terms of capacity range and cell format.

We performed a correlation analysis similar to the one in Section 4 whose results are shown in Fig. 11. It reveals that the frequency range with the highest correlation with temperature is shifted towards lower frequencies compared to those previously analysed, with the strongest correlation observed at 90Hz.

The Denso spectra are too few for inclusion in the previous analysis: they would behave as outliers. Some changes in the results were already expected for such larger cells with a different format and chemistry. However, the approach is still valid, provided a new training on the new dataset. Using only the Denso dataset, the model yields an RMSE of 2.51°C: the model still captures the behaviour of large-capacity cells.

In perspective, our model could be improved by including the battery capacity as a new input variable. Reaching such a goal would require the acquisition of new data, covering a wide range of cell capacities. This would enable the study of changes in the electrochemical dynamics through the impedance and their impact on the model performance. From a practical point of view, some steps should be repeated as a systematic approach to generate the model: (i) the correlation analysis of Section 4, (ii) the definition of the best frequency range, (iii) the model training at different frequencies in the chosen range, and (iv) the analysis of the RMSE to find the best frequency (see Section 5).

Going beyond, a similar approach could be adopted to widen the model or generate a new model using data from cells having various shapes and chemistries, possibly including also LFP. Indeed, despite the widespread use of LFP batteries in applications, they are still missing from the analysis presented above, due to the lack of available experimental data.
In this paper, we presented the identification of a model for battery temperature estimation based on impedance features like the resistance, reactance, magnitude and phase of the impedance.
A correlation analysis showed that the reactance at frequencies over 100Hz is the most suitable temperature indicator, with a correlation index over 0.95.

We evaluated the performance of 48 linear and non-linear models for temperature estimation, including combinations of the normalised reactance of the impedance at different frequencies, SoC and SoH as inputs. The evaluation was done through a cross-validation approach on 2064 EIS spectra coming from four different datasets containing the data from experiments on several Li-ion cells with different technologies, similar capacities and performed under multiple SoC, SoH, offset current and temperature conditions. One of the datasets collects some results of an experimental campaign performed within a collaboration between the University of Salerno and Kiel University.

An exponential relationship with the reactance at 335Hz, was the best one, with the lowest rms error. The results of the fitting analysis also showed that the temperature estimation improved by introducing in the model information on SoC and SoH in linear terms. The temperature estimation, achieved with an rms error within 2°C, works for different chemistries, and battery states.

These results consent to state that the temperature effect over the battery impedance has a common behaviour among different Li-ion batteries. The reactance in the frequency range 100:1000Hz is immune to bias currents at least until C/4, and could be used as an indicator for battery temperature estimation.

These tests are aimed to measure the actual capacity Q of the cell at each temperature
T
amb

Fig. A.1 depicts the trend of battery capacity with temperature. The box-and-wiskers plot shows the mean value of the capacity, and the spread and skewness groups of the capacity measurements through their quartiles. The red lines represent the mean value, the boxes represent the spread of the capacity from the 25th to the 75th percentile. Additionally, the upper and lower lines, called wiskers, represent the maximum and minimum values. In the range from -10°C to 20°C, the capacity grows almost linearly with temperature from 78%
Q
n
